preFix.title: Livestock market day: what to check before you go and when you arrive
preFix.subtitle: Paperwork, health checks, and the things that catch out first-time buyers.
preFix.excerpt: Buying livestock at market is fast and often bewildering the first time. The animals move quickly through the ring, the auctioneers speak their own shorthand, and the paperwork requirements are strict. A guide to the pre-market checks, the documentation you must have in place, and the key things to look for in the ring on the day.
preFix.sourceNotes: Rural Payments Agency, CPH number guidance, https://www.gov.uk/guidance/get-a-county-parish-holding-number. DEFRA guidance on livestock movement records, Crown copyright. AHDB, buying and moving livestock factsheets, open-access.

content.text: Buying livestock at market is a practical and often cost-effective way to start a flock or expand an existing one. The speed of the auction ring means that preparation before you arrive matters more than decisions made in the moment. Knowing what the paperwork requires, what health signs to look for, and how the movement records work prevents the most common mistakes.

before you purchase any sheep, goats, cattle, or pigs at market. Buyers without a CPH number cannot legally take livestock away. Apply online to the Rural Payments Agency; the process takes a couple of days and is free. Some markets ask to see your CPH number at registration.

For sheep and goats, you will need a flock or herd mark before your animals can be ear-tagged on your holding. This is issued by APHA after your CPH is registered. You do not need it on the day you buy, but you will need it before you move animals off your holding.

attrs.body: Auction fever is real. Setting a price limit per head before you go stops you bidding beyond what makes financial sense. Write the limit on your hand if necessary. The auctioneer moves fast and there is no pause to reconsider.
attrs.title: Set a firm budget before you enter the ring

content.text: At the market: what to look for

Arrive early enough to walk through the pens before the sale starts. An hour before sale time is usually enough. Look at each animal from a distance first: stance, alertness, and how it moves. A sheep sitting separately from the group, a goat with its back hunched, or a pig lying flat and unresponsive are signs of illness.

Look at eyes, nose, and feet in the pen. Clear eyes and a dry nose suggest a healthy animal. Cloudy eyes may indicate pinkeye or nutritional deficiency. Watery or crusty discharge around the nose is a sign of respiratory infection. A sheep standing on three legs or shifting weight is carrying a foot problem.

Condition scoring by eye takes time to learn, but a rough guide: you should not be able to see individual ribs, and the backbone should not be sharp and prominent. Animals sold in very thin condition may carry a disease burden, a worm load, or a dental problem that explains their condition.
The auctioneer's staff will produce a

at the market office. Check it before leaving: it should carry the vendor's CPH number, your CPH number, the number and species of animals, and the date of movement. Keep this for your holding register. You are required by law to record the arrival in your register within 36 hours.
When they arrive on your holding

starts the moment your new livestock arrive on the holding. Six days for sheep, goats, and cattle; 20 days for pigs. During this period no other sheep, goats, or cattle can leave your holding (even those that arrived before). Plan your movements around this; if you have animals going to a show or to the abattoir, arrange it before the new animals arrive.

House new animals separately for at least two weeks before mixing with your established flock or herd. New arrivals may carry parasites, respiratory infections, or skin conditions not obvious at the time of purchase. A quarantine pen with its own water and hay, out of nose contact with the existing animals, protects your existing stock.

items.fix: Isolate immediately if you suspect respiratory or gut infection. Contact your vet. Do not mix with existing stock until the issue is resolved.
items.cause: Stress of transport and change of environment, or existing illness present at purchase
items.symptom: Animals showing signs of illness within 48 hours of arriving

items.fix: Return to the market and request a corrected document before leaving. You cannot legally record the movement in your register without a valid movement document.
items.cause: Market office error or informal sale made without proper paperwork
items.symptom: Movement document is incomplete or seller's CPH is missing

items.fix: Keep in a well-secured yard pen for the first two days while they settle. Run a temporary electric strand along the existing fence line. Some animals bought from intensive systems have never seen electric fencing and need to learn it.
items.cause: Unfamiliar fencing type, or animals experienced at breaking through it
items.symptom: Newly purchased stock immediately breaking out of existing fencing
